sentences.text: Apparently, one of the conditions under which these countries allowed us in-- or at least Belgium-- was that we wouldn't work-- that the men wouldn't work.
Because I guess they were afraid that they would take jobs away from the indigenous population.
But anyway, he didn't-- I-- I assumed he could have worked.
Tailors are needed everywhere.
But he didn't.
Oh, the Jewish organizations in Europe-- I, guess the one in Brussels and Belgium-- helped us and found apartments for us.
And, I think, also gave us some money so that we could, you know, eat and just subsist.
And so we lived there for almost a year.
My father continued to try to get our visa to the United States.
We were able to correspond with our relatives in the United States and in Cuba and back in Poland.
And so, you know, there were-- there were all the connections.
It's possible that we-- some people in the United States-- some relatives-- sent us some money.
I don't know.
I do know that when we got back-- when we finally got to the United States, we had a lot of debts.
So maybe they did send us some money.
But we were able to subsist.
I just remember I hated school.
I just hated it.
Was there anti-Semitism in the school there?
No.
I don't remember any anti-Semitism, per se.
I just-- I think I was unhappy because-- I mean, I know I was unhappy because I didn't have any friends and I didn't speak the language.
And somehow, I didn't-- it took me a long time to pick it up, apparently.
Because-- I don't know why.
I don't know why.
In any case, we landed in Belgium on June 17 of 1939.
And the Second World War broke out on September 1, 1939, when Germany invaded Poland.
And a couple, or three days later, England and France declared war on Germany because they had this treaty with Poland.
So there was war.
But the war-- there was no active war on the Western border of Germany.
They were invading Poland.
And-- and they-- just before that, apparently, an agreement had been made with Russia that Germany would take half of Poland, Russia the other half.
Anyway, the-- the Germans overwhelmed Poland pretty rapidly and, unfortunately, that included all my relatives who were-- who were in Poland.
They were in the part of Poland that was nearest Germany.
They were near Krakow.
And-- but it-- as it turned out, it didn't really make any difference where in Poland you were.
You were at great risk.
So we-- we-- the war was going on, but we were in Belgium.
Belgium was neutral.
It was not at war so we felt fairly safe.
However, on May 10 of 1940-- almost a year after we got to Belgium-- the Germans attacked France.
And they attacked France directly by trying to invade France, but also through Holland and Belgium.
Didn't matter that those two latter countries were neutral.
The Germans didn't care.
I think they did the same thing in the First World War, too.
Anyway, they attacked through Belgium and Holland.
And we were in Brussels and I remember, you know, the-- the air raid sirens would sound.
I remember one time, we had been visiting some friends and we were walking back to our room when the air siren found-- sounded.
And there was no air raid shelter around.
I don't even know if they had air raid shelters in Belgium.
But anyway, we sort of ducked into a doorway.
And we could hear the bombs dropping and we could see the explosions.
And this happened several times.
Anyway, we-- on the-- this invasion-- the blitzkrieg-- began on the 10th.
On the 14th-- the night of the 14th-- my father said to my mother, listen-- and by that time, we knew that the Germans were maybe 20 miles outside of Brussels.
He said, you know, we've got to get out of here.
We've got to flee.
And the only place to flee was to France.
I mean, we didn't know what the-- the military situation was, which, if we knew, we would have probably stayed put, which would have been terrible.
But anyway, we-- so the next morning, we got up very early and we and several other families decided that we were going to walk to France.
I mean, there was no transport.
Who had cars?
Cars were a rarity in those days, anyway.
We certainly didn't have a car.
So the only way to go was to-- to-- to walk.
My father took a sheet.
He put some of our possessions in it and he slung it over his shoulder and we started walking.
And as luck would have it, we passed a railroad station.
Now, you couldn't get a train because all the trains were being used for troop movements.
But as we were passing the-- the train station, there was a rumor that there was an empty train which was going to France.
So we-- we-- and it seemed like hundreds or thousands of others-- got-- you know, got into the train station.
We-- we-- we were-- we found out which train, supposedly, it was.
And I remember my father physically fighting-- pushing and fighting other people to get us on this train.
And we actually-- we got on the train.
And it went.
It actually went towards France.
That was in the morning of the 15th of May.
And we got to the border-- the French-Belgian border sometime in the late afternoon.
And then the train stopped.
We had-- we were told we had to get out.
So we get out.
And we-- no sooner had we gotten out of the train than the air raid sirens sounded.
And they told us to go into the air raid shelters and we went.
Now, there were hundreds of people-- I don't know, maybe thousands-- who were on this platform.
And everybody pushed into the shelter.
And I don't know how long this air raid-- this particular air raid lasted.
But, you know, there was no oxygen.
There were too many people in this shelter.
And it was-- it became difficult to breathe.
And finally, the all clear sounded and, you know, everybody rushed out.
And-- and, you know, we could breathe again.
But about three or four minutes later, the air raid sirens started again.
And I remember my father saying to my mother, listen, Paula, we're not going in there again.
If we're going to die, we'll die here on the platform.
We're not going to suffocate to death down there.
So we stayed on this railroad platform through the evening and through the night.
And I really remember that very well because the air raids kept-- you know, there was an air raid.
You'd hear the siren.
And then a few minutes later, there was an all clear.
And then a few minutes later, the-- the siren would start again.
I mean, this went on all night.
And of course, at night, you couldn't see the planes.
But you could hear the whistling of the bombs as they were falling.
And you didn't know whether that whistle was-- was for you.
You didn't know where that bomb was going to fall.
You just heard the whistle.
Now, you may have seen movies in which there are planes and they drop bombs and you hear the whistle and so on.
It doesn't mean much to you.
But when you're there and the bomb is falling, perhaps, on you, it's-- it is horrifying.
We were lucky.
And we-- we figured that, probably, the Germans would try to bomb the railroad station because at least that was a military objective.
And-- and I'm not sure whether they actually hit part of the railroad station or not.
But where we were, nothing was hit.
I remember, also-- apparently, the Belgians or the French were-- were shooting at the German planes.
And I remember they must have hit one because I saw this flame in the sky and the flame coming down.
All very, very scary.
Well, you know, we were-- we were extremely lucky.
Or you might say God was with us.
Because none of those bombs hit us.
And the next morning, we were told that the train would continue.
So we got on, back on the train.
And we continued towards Paris, as it turned out.
Now, I didn't know this.
I read it later-- many, many years later when I read Churchill's memoirs.
But on the day that we were leaving the French border-- I think the name of the town was Lille-- no-- Amiens, Amiens-- for Paris.
The Germans-- they were doing a pincer movement and they were about to cross the railroad as we were going to Paris and cut it off.
And I think, from what I read, we must have just gotten through, maybe, hours before they actually cut that railroad off.

But what I remember most about that trip from Amiens to Paris was that, repeatedly, the train stopped and we were told to get out of the train and lie in the field because there were German planes overhead-- flying overhead.
Well, the first time, I guess the conductor must have come around and said, get out, Germans overhead.
But after that, as soon as the train stopped, everybody knew.
We just ran out and we lay on the field.
And I remember I looked up and I saw these planes just flying there.
But they-- they-- the Germans must have realized that this was not a troop train because they saw-- you know, they could see, I'm sure, that we were just civilians.
But-- but we didn't know.
We-- they could have-- I mean, they could've machined us-- machine gunned us all.
We were just lying there.
And-- and-- or they could have bombed the train, in which case, that would have been that.
But it didn't-- things didn't happen.
I remember-- you know, we-- we-- we'd run out of the train.
We'd lie down on the field.
And my mother would sort of cover me so that if-- if, God forbid, there was some shooting, she would get it, not me.
[SIGHS] Anyway, we-- again, by some miracle-- we got to Paris.
And it was evening.
We stayed overnight.
And of course, Paris was bedlam.
The next morning-- you know, we-- we-- some people decided that they were going to stay in Paris.
But my father said, no, we're going to get as far away from the Germans as possible.
So we were looking-- we wanted to find a train that was going farther south.
And we were told the next morning that there was this train going south.
It was a cattle car.
Well, we got on the cattle car with a lot of other people.
And the train went south.
Now, that part of the trip was-- the Germans weren't flying overhead, as far as I remember.
And we just kept going.
We kept going.
And we went as far as we could, which was practically to the Spanish border, in the Pyrenees.
Now of course, it wasn't only Jews that were fleeing.
There were a lot of people who were fleeing from the battle, from the war.
And we got to this town called [NON-ENGLISH].
We got off.
I think we had to get off.
I think it was the last stop before this-- before Spain.
Of course, we didn't have a Spanish passport so we couldn't go to Spain.
And so there were a bunch of refugees.
I don't know how many there were.
And the French started, you know, distributing us among the town and various villages around there.
And we ended up in a village about two kilometers, or three kilometers from the town, called Savignac.
And-- and-- and the townpeople were very nice.
They-- they-- there were some empty rooms or empty houses and they allowed us to stay there.
I mean, you know, it was war.
We were refugees.
They didn't know from Jews.
They didn't care, you know, except that they knew that we weren't from France because we didn't speak French.
I spoke-- by that time, I guess I spoke very little.
My mother spoke a little, too, because they'd spoken French in-- in Brussels.
So we settled-- we settled down in this little town-- village.
It was a village of maybe 200 people.
But I remember this village.
I could draw you a map and tell you exactly where everybody lived.
I mean, I remember it that well.
Because we lived there for two years.
But anyway-- [SIGHS] about-- I guess it was October.
So it's three months after we got there.
The French police came and they picked up all the Jewish refugees.
And they took us to a detention camp.
Now, the detention camp we were taken to was in Agde.
It's near the Mediterranean-- near the city of Montpelier.
It was quite a ways from where we were and we were put there.
Now, this was not-- you know, it was a detention camp.
It was not a concentration camp.
I think what it was, originally, were barracks-- military barracks which weren't being used.
And we were put in there.
Now, as I said, it wasn't-- it wasn't like other concentration camps in Poland.
But it was terrible enough.
We had-- I mean, there were beds or cots or anything like that.
They put hay out and we took hay and we-- we slept on it.
We slept in our clothes.
Very soon, we started itching because we had lice.
And this was-- this was just everybody.
And there were, I don't know how many people in this one barrack that we were in.
I-- I don't know.
At least-- at least 100.
It was a long barrack.
But you know, and people-- [SIGHS] people started fighting over the amount of straw.
I mean, it was-- it was so, so bad.
My mother and I-- the women and children were in one part of the camp and the men were in the other-- in another part of the camp.
The men, I guess, worked or did some kind of labor.
So I was with my mother.
And once a week, we were allowed to approach a fence, which-- which separated the men's area from the women's area and-- and so my mother could talk to my father.
And I could talk to my father.
I don't think I mentioned it.
My mother's name is Paula-- or her Jew-- Yiddish name is [?
Pesha. ?] She was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And my father's name was Zalman.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, we were-- oh, yes.
I was telling you about what I remember about this-- this camp.
The food was terrible.
I mean, I remember we would get, for lunch or supper-- I don't know.
We would get this thin soup with a piece of hard bread.
That's-- that was what we ate.
I mean, we were constantly starving.
But the worst was the-- the-- the bathroom.
I mean, there was one huge outdoor--
Yeah, latrine.
It was huge and-- and it was almost impossible to approach because it smelled so terrible.
But, you know, I mean, what could you do?
You weren't-- you couldn't-- you couldn't do these-- the things that you needed to in the bathroom anywhere else.
And so you had to go.
And I just-- the smell was unbelievable.
I was-- the French took us.
The French police came-- the gendarme-- and they arrested us.
And they took us to this camp.
Oh, yeah.
Sure.
No reason.
We were refugees.
We were Jewish.
Did they need a reason?
They didn't-- I mean, I don't specifically remember.
I guess they just said you have to come with us.
This was the French, not the Germans.
The French.
I always thought that they-- that the French did that at the behest of the Germans.
That they arrested the Jews and put them in detention camps.
But from what I read later, when I was an adult, that wasn't so.
The French did it all on their own.
They-- you have to remember that the Germans actually physically took over the north-- the northern part of France.
They occupied that part of France.
That was called Occupied France.
And the southern part of France was sort of set up as a puppet state to the Germans.
It was called Vichy France because the capital was in Vichy.
That was in eastern France.
And Petain was that President.
Petain was a hero from the First World War.
He certainly wasn't a hero when he became president of Vichy France.
But apparently, it was the-- the-- the French.

And I think the Germans-- of course, the Germans must have picked out the people who were going to be in this free-- quote, 'free-- free French government.' And they must have picked out people who were on the right or Nazis and so on.
So you know, they were the ones who directed the arrest and resettlement of the Jewish refugees in these-- in these detention camps.
It was not at the behest of the Germans.
They did it themselves-- the French.
Well, apparently some-- an underground formed in-- around this camp and maybe other camps, too.
And the-- to help people escape.
My father heard about this and during one of the weekly times that we could meet, he told my mother about it and he said he was trying to arrange for my mother and me to-- to-- to escape.
And finally, we were told we-- we could-- we would be escaping.
And we did, as a matter of fact.
My mother and I-- it was on Christmas Eve.
The French soldiers were drunk and so it was sort of easy.
[DRINKS] And-- but my father-- of course, he was in a separate part of the camp and he-- my mother didn't want to go because she knew that he was going to stay there.
But he insisted.
He said-- he said to her-- I mean, I was standing there.
I remember.
He said to her, if you don't go, you'll have your child's blood on your hands.
So she-- she went.
She-- we went.
And it was in the morning of Christmas Eve, I guess.
Because I remember we got to the--
[SIGHS] How we got to this town of Agde from the camp, I don't remember.
But I remember we-- and they told us that we should-- they gave us a train ticket and they told us to take the train.
And they said-- they told us, well, go back to the town where you were because there was no other place for us to go.
We didn't know anybody in France.
I-- I don't-- I really don't know.
My father may have told us.
But anyway, we-- we-- and it wasn't just my mother and I.
There were three-- [COUGHS] three other people who were with us.
We-- [COUGHS] Excuse me.
We got to the railroad station and-- and there was a train there.
And we started-- we got on the platform.
And suddenly, I saw a couple of soldiers whom I recognized from the camp.
And I said to my mother and the people who were there, those soldiers are from the camp.
They might recognize us.
So we ran out of the train station and we went to a cafe.
And we went inside, as far back as we could, and we sat there for hours.
We asked when the next train out was and they told us it was in the evening.
So we sat there and, of course, we were scared.
We thought, my god.
How are we ever going to get on this train if they have these gendarme there all the time?
Anyway, when it came time for the next train to leave, we left the coffee house.
I'm sure the proprietor must have known who we were because we weren't speaking French and, you know, the way we looked.
But he was-- he didn't say anything, obviously.
Anyway, we got on the platform of the railroad station.
There were no gendarme.
We got on the-- the train and we-- we took the train back to the town-- the village where we had lived.
Now, you know, I make it sound simple.
It was very complicated.
We had tickets, all right, for the train.
But we didn't have-- you needed travel documents.
You couldn't just get on a train and go.
This was during the war.
You needed a permission-- a document that-- that-- that allowed you to go.
But we didn't have that.
Well, what choice did we have?
We got on the train and we prayed that nobody would check the documents and nobody-- nobody, in fact, did.
Again, you know, we were-- it-- it was just-- it was just a-- a series of miracles is really the only way I can-- I can look at this.
So anyway, we-- we got to this town.
Yes, another thing that I remember.
The-- there were-- there was this-- we were in this village.
The town nearby-- [NON-ENGLISH]-- was the main railroad station so we thought we would go there.
But then the adults started worrying that there would be policemen there.
You know, it was a town of some size.
It wasn't big, but it was a town.

So we knew that there was another stop-- that the train would stop not at our little village, but at a village on the other side of-- there would be this-- the village, then our village, and then the town.
So we decided we'd get off in this other village.
Now, this was-- this-- remember, this was Christmas.
And this was in the Pyrenees.
And I-- the only shoes that I had were these sabots-- these wooden shoes.
And I didn't have heavy socks or anything like that.
We didn't have heavy clothes.
And we got off at this little village-- not in the town because we were afraid of the gendarme being there.
And we started walking to the village.
Well, it was freezing.
It was in the mountains.
It was high in the mountains.
There was snow on the ground.
And we had to walk-- I don't know-- maybe a couple of miles.
And we finally got to the village where we had stayed.
And we-- the first house we-- we went in-- we had befriended this woman.
Madame Crouveiller was her name.
Funny how-- the things you remember.
And we went in and she had-- she saw us and she welcomed us.
And she took us in.
She had a fire going.
And when my feet warmed up again, I-- I-- I didn't realize-- my mother realized, because I kept telling her my toes hurt, that they had been frostbitten.
And fortunately, it wasn't a severe frostbite.
But-- and so nothing happened.
Except that, even today, when I-- when it's very cold outside or when I go skiing-- when it's gets very cold, I can feel those toes that were frostbitten.
I still-- they still hurt when they get really cold.
